items.title: OpenAI Codex can now drive macOS apps while your Mac is locked
items.summary: OpenAI shipped a feature called 'Locked Use' for the Codex desktop app on macOS, announced May 21 and covered widely through May 24. It builds on Codex's Computer Use plugin — the part that lets the coding agent actually operate a graphical app rather than just call an API: once you grant Screen Recording and Accessibility permissions, Codex can click through windows, type, navigate menus, and use the clipboard in apps you explicitly allow. The new piece is an Apple authorization plug-in that temporarily unlocks the Mac during an active, trusted task, so you can kick off and monitor a long-running agent run remotely from your phone while the machine sits locked on your desk. The lock screen's local-access protections stay on the whole time, and if Codex detects real keyboard or mouse activity it immediately re-locks the Mac and pauses remote access until you manually unlock it again. It explicitly can't automate Terminal apps, Codex itself, or system-level admin prompts, and it's unavailable in the EEA, UK, and Switzerland at launch. It's part of a broader push to turn Codex into a full desktop agent — watching the screen, driving apps, and running from mobile. Reported by Macworld, MacRumors, MacObserver, and TechTimes.
items.why_it_matters: You already run an agent that edits files and runs commands (Claude Code); Codex is OpenAI's equivalent, and this is that same idea pushed one step further — the agent driving a real GUI, unattended, on a machine that's locked and out of your hands. The detail worth studying isn't the convenience, it's the permission model. The agent doesn't get a blanket unlock; it gets a temporary, task-scoped unlock that snaps shut the moment a human touches the keyboard, and it's walled off from Terminal and admin prompts entirely. That's a concrete example of the kind of boundary you'd want around any agent you let near your computer — and it pairs directly with this morning's Laravel supply-chain item, where the whole risk was an agent doing installs unattended with no human in the loop. The theme of the week is agents acting on their own, and the interesting engineering question is what scaffolding makes that safe. For your HTML/CSS/Python work the takeaway is less 'go use this' and more 'this is what GUI-driving agents look like in practice, and here's a worked example of the guardrails.'
items.why_it_matters_brief: OpenAI's Codex can now operate apps on a locked Mac, triggered remotely from your phone — a coding agent running unattended behind the lock screen, with a scoped temporary-unlock model.

takes.take: Frames it as the agent no longer needing an unlocked machine to keep working while you're away, and notes the Apple authorization plug-in that brokers the temporary unlock.
Details the safeguards — the lock screen's local protections stay active, and any detected keyboard or mouse activity instantly re-locks the Mac and pauses remote access.
Places Locked Use inside the larger shift of Codex into a full desktop agent that controls Mac apps, watches the screen, and runs from mobile.

items.title: Anthropic is closing a $30B round at a $900B+ valuation — passing OpenAI for the first time
items.summary: Per Bloomberg (May 22), Anthropic is set to close a funding round of more than $30 billion as soon as the end of this week, at a pre-money valuation above $900 billion. That would put it ahead of OpenAI's $852 billion March 2026 valuation, making Anthropic the most valuable private AI company for the first time — a sharp reversal from February 2026, when it was valued at $380 billion. The round is co-led by Sequoia, Dragoneer, Greenoaks, and Altimeter, each putting in roughly $2 billion, with existing backers including Peter Thiel's Founders Fund and General Catalyst also participating. It lands the same week Anthropic projected $10.9 billion in Q2 revenue and its first-ever quarterly operating profit, and the same day as Pope Leo XIV's AI encyclical, which Anthropic co-founder Chris Olah co-presented at the Vatican (in this morning's section). No term sheet has been signed yet and terms could still change. Coverage: Bloomberg, Yahoo Finance, TechTimes, and The Next Web.
items.why_it_matters: For someone who builds on Claude rather than invests in it, the number that matters underneath the headline valuation is the $30 billion of fresh capital, because in this business that converts almost directly into compute. A raise this size is a multi-year war chest for GPUs, TPUs, and the custom-silicon buildout behind the API you call — it's what funds the next Claude models and the per-token price cuts that come from running inference at scale (it ties straight to the Anthropic-Microsoft Maia-200 chip talks in this morning's section). It also reorders the field: Anthropic passing OpenAI on valuation, days after its first profitable quarter, means the lab you rely on is now the most richly funded in the space. The honest caveat is that a private valuation is a negotiated number, not revenue, and skeptics (Ed Zitron's 'profitability swindle' read, flagged earlier this week) argue these figures lean on circular cloud-and-chip commitments. Treat it as the business backdrop to every Claude release, not as something to act on.
items.why_it_matters_brief: Anthropic is closing a $30B round at a $900B+ valuation, passing OpenAI for the first time — the compute war chest behind the next Claude models and the API you build on.

takes.take: Leads on the milestone itself — a $900B+ valuation would make Anthropic the most valuable AI startup, surpassing OpenAI for the first time.
Notes the round could stretch well beyond $30B and frames the $900B figure against how fast Anthropic's valuation has climbed through 2026.
